title: Evaluating Across the Lifecycle
concept: Use loss/perplexity in pretraining, benchmark suites (MMLU) in tuning, safety audits (red-teaming) in alignment, and user feedback in production.

exercises.prompt: When do engineers evaluate an AI model's performance?

options.text: Only on the very last day before launch
Only when the AI starts writing its own code

exercises.explain: Evaluating an AI is a continuous process that changes at every step of its development, from basic code-level math to real human interactions.

cards.title: Testing at Every Step
cards.body: How do we know if an AI is ready for the real world? We don't just test it once! An AI must pass completely different tests as it grows from a baby model reading the internet to a finished app helping millions of users.

cards.title: Evaluation Across the Lifecycle
cards.body: Evaluating an AI isn't a one-time test. We use different metrics at each stage: Loss/Perplexity measures raw learning during Pretraining; Benchmark Suites (like MMLU) test general skills during Tuning; Safety Audits (red-teaming) check for harms during Alignment; and User Feedback monitors real-world helpfulness in Production.

cards.title: The Athlete's Journey
cards.body: Think of an AI like a professional athlete. During basic gym training, you measure their strength (loss/perplexity). In tryouts, you test them on standard drills (benchmarks). In safety trials, you check if they play fair (red-teaming). Finally, in the actual league games, the crowd's reaction tells you how they are doing (user feedback).

cards.title: Evaluating a Medical Assistant
cards.body:
Imagine training a medical AI helper:
1. Pretraining: You measure 'perplexity' to make sure it understands basic medical terms.
2. Tuning: You run it through 'MMLU' to see if it can pass a biology exam.
3. Alignment: Expert doctors try to trick it into giving dangerous prescriptions (red-teaming) to fix safety flaws.
4. Production: Real doctors give thumbs up/down on its advice daily.

cards.title: The Benchmark Trap
cards.body: Many people think that if an AI gets a perfect score on a school benchmark (like MMLU), it is completely safe and ready to launch. In reality, a smart AI can still give harmful advice or fail in production without safety red-teaming and user feedback!

cards.title: Math Scores vs. Human Value
cards.body: The focus of evaluation shifts dramatically as the AI moves from early training to final release.

right.text: Measures human safety, helpfulness, and real-world user satisfaction.

left.text: Measures mathematical prediction score (Loss & Perplexity) on raw text data.

exercises.prompt: At which stage of the AI lifecycle do engineers primarily look at loss and perplexity?
exercises.explain: Perplexity and loss measure how well the AI predicts the next word, which is the primary goal during the initial pretraining stage.

exercises.prompt: Is user feedback collected during the production phase of the AI lifecycle?
exercises.explain: User feedback (like thumbs up/down buttons) is collected in the production stage when the AI is live and being used by real people.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing stage of evaluation used for safety audits:
exercises.sentence: We use loss in pretraining, benchmarks in tuning, [blank] in alignment, and user feedback in production.
exercises.explain: During the alignment stage, safety audits (often called red-teaming) are used to actively find and fix weaknesses before the AI reaches the public.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a Benchmark Suite evaluation during the tuning phase?

options.text: Model A: A tester trying to make the AI explain how to build a lockpick.
Model B: An automated script scoring the AI on 10,000 chemistry and history questions.
Model C: A regular user clicking the 'dislike' button on a slow chat response.

exercises.explain: Model B is behaving like an automated benchmark test suite (like MMLU) which tests general knowledge across multiple choice questions, rather than safety probing or live usage feedback.

exercises.prompt: Order the evaluation methods correctly from the earliest stage of an AI's development to its final stage: Benchmark Suites, Loss/Perplexity, User Feedback, Safety Audits.
exercises.explain: Throughout its lifecycle, an AI is evaluated using different metrics: raw loss during pretraining, benchmark tests during tuning, safety audits before release, and user satisfaction once live in production.
